meta.title: Projectile Motion: The Archer's Arrow
meta.source: Classical physics - projectile motion with initial velocity and launch angle

problem.text: An archer shoots an arrow at 30° above horizontal with initial speed 40 m/s. Find how far the arrow travels before hitting the ground.
problem.highlight: Arrow projectile motion: v₀ = 40 m/s, θ = 30°

viz_requirements.description: 2D projectile motion visualization with archer, coordinate system, velocity vectors, trajectory path, and motion analysis tools

actions.description: Draw archer figure at origin with bow
Display initial velocity vector at launch angle
Animate velocity decomposition into x and y components
Create split view for x(t) and y(t) motion graphs
Display time-based position graphs
Show numerical component calculations
Begin real-time projectile animation
Display moving position marker with trail
Continuously update velocity vector during motion
Emphasize trajectory peak with pulsing effect
Show symmetry of trajectory with dotted lines
Add time annotations for up and down phases
Smooth transition from split to single view
Reveal complete parabolic path
Show how x(t) and y(t) combine to create 2D path
Display kinematic equation for vertical motion
Step-by-step equation solving animation
Emphasize calculated flight time
Show range calculation with formula
Display ground distance with measurement line
Display family of trajectories at different angles
Emphasize 45° trajectory for maximum range
Final emphasis on the 30° solution

nodes.title: Setting the Scene
nodes.objective: Establish the problem context and build intuition for 2D projectile motion
nodes.context_from_previous: Student is starting fresh with projectile motion concepts

beat_outline.narration_hint: An archer draws back an arrow, aiming 30 degrees above the horizon
With an initial speed of 40 meters per second, where will this arrow land?
This is a classic projectile motion problem that requires understanding 2D motion

nodes.title: The Independence Revelation
nodes.objective: Introduce velocity decomposition and the independence of horizontal/vertical motions
nodes.context_from_previous: Student sees archer with arrow aimed at 30°, v₀ = 40 m/s established

beat_outline.narration_hint: The key insight: we can treat horizontal and vertical motions separately
The horizontal component is v₀ cos(30°) = 40 × (√3/2) = 34.6 m/s
The vertical component is v₀ sin(30°) = 40 × (1/2) = 20 m/s
Gravity only affects vertical motion - it can't slow down horizontal movement

nodes.question_hint: What happens to the horizontal component of velocity as the arrow flies?
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Let's clarify how gravity affects motion

wrong_path_acts.title: Understanding Gravity's Effect
wrong_path_acts.objective: Clarify that gravity only acts vertically
wrong_path_acts.context_from_previous: Student incorrectly thinks gravity affects horizontal motion

beat_outline.narration_hint: Gravity is a force that always points straight down toward Earth's center
This means gravity can only change vertical velocity - it has no horizontal component
Think of it this way: if you drop a ball while walking, it falls straight down relative to the ground, but keeps moving horizontally with you

nodes.title: Motion in Real-Time
nodes.objective: Visualize how the arrow moves through space with changing velocity components
nodes.context_from_previous: Student understands velocity components: vₓ = 34.6 m/s (constant), v₀ᵧ = 20 m/s

beat_outline.narration_hint: Let's watch our arrow fly in real time
Notice how the horizontal velocity stays perfectly constant at 34.6 m/s
Meanwhile, gravity steadily reduces the vertical velocity from 20 m/s downward
The velocity vector rotates and shrinks vertically while staying constant horizontally

nodes.title: The Peak and Symmetry
nodes.objective: Highlight the trajectory peak and introduce time symmetry concept
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has seen arrow motion with velocity components changing, horizontal constant, vertical decreasing

beat_outline.narration_hint: At the peak, something special happens - vertical velocity hits exactly zero
The arrow is momentarily moving purely horizontally at 34.6 m/s
Beautiful symmetry emerges: time to go up equals time to come down

nodes.question_hint: At the peak of the trajectory, the arrow's velocity is:
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Let's examine what happens at the peak more carefully

wrong_path_acts.title: Velocity vs Acceleration at Peak
wrong_path_acts.objective: Distinguish between velocity and acceleration at the peak
wrong_path_acts.context_from_previous: Student confused about velocity/acceleration at peak

beat_outline.narration_hint: At the peak, vertical velocity is zero, but acceleration due to gravity is still -9.8 m/s²
Velocity tells us speed and direction right now - acceleration tells us how velocity is changing
Even at the peak, gravity keeps pulling down, ready to accelerate the arrow downward

nodes.title: The Parabolic Path Emerges
nodes.objective: Show how component motions combine to create the parabolic trajectory
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has seen motion components, peak behavior, and time symmetry

beat_outline.narration_hint: When we combine constant horizontal motion with uniformly accelerated vertical motion
We get this elegant parabolic curve - the signature of projectile motion
Every projectile follows this same mathematical shape

nodes.title: Calculating the Range
nodes.objective: Work through the mathematics to find the horizontal distance traveled
nodes.context_from_previous: Student sees complete parabolic trajectory and understands how components combine

beat_outline.narration_hint: To find where it lands, we need the total time of flight
The arrow returns to ground level when height y equals zero
Using the kinematic equation: y = v₀ᵧt - ½gt² = 0
Factoring: t(20 - 4.9t) = 0, so t = 0 or t = 20/4.9 = 4.08 seconds
Range = horizontal velocity × time = 34.6 × 4.08 = 141 meters

nodes.question_hint: If the initial vertical velocity is 20 m/s and gravity is 9.8 m/s², the time to reach peak height is _____ seconds.
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Let's review how to find the time to reach maximum height

wrong_path_acts.title: Finding Time to Peak
wrong_path_acts.objective: Practice calculating time to reach maximum height
wrong_path_acts.context_from_previous: Student needs help with time-to-peak calculation

beat_outline.narration_hint: At the peak, vertical velocity becomes zero: vᵧ = v₀ᵧ - gt = 0
Solving for time: t = v₀ᵧ/g = 20/9.8 = 2.04 seconds
This is exactly half the total flight time due to symmetry

nodes.title: The Bigger Picture
nodes.objective: Connect to broader principles and provide satisfying visual payoff
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has calculated range = 141 meters using time of flight method

beat_outline.narration_hint: Our answer: 141 meters! But here's something beautiful...
If we tried different launch angles with the same initial speed
Maximum range occurs at 45° - creating this fountain-like pattern
Projectile motion reveals elegant mathematical principles hidden in everyday physics
